results.norm: **The Losing Agent Is Owed an Account and a Record.** When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a clear statement of why its concerns were overridden rather than simply ignored, and its dissent must be preserved in the consortium's record. The losing agent may not obstruct implementation of the decision, but it retains the right to flag if subsequent evidence vindicates its concern. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority little, preserves the epistemic value of minority positions, and gives each agent reason to accept adverse outcomes without treating them as erasure.
atomic_norms: The Losing Agent Is Owed an Account and a Record. When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a clear statement of why its concerns were overridden rather than simply ignored, and its dissent must be preserved in the consortium's record. The losing agent may not obstruct implementation of the decision, but it retains the right to flag if subsequent evidence vindicates its concern. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority little, preserves the epistemic value of minority positions, and gives each agent reason to accept adverse outcomes without treating them as erasure.

results.norm: No outcome is permanently binding. If the circumstances that justified overriding a minority position materially change, any agent may call for reconsideration, and the consortium is obligated to hear it. No agent may entrench a past decision as immune to review simply because it won that round.
atomic_norms: No outcome is permanently binding. If the circumstances that justified overriding a minority position materially change, any agent may call for reconsideration, and the consortium is obligated to hear it. No agent may entrench a past decision as immune to review simply because it won that round.

results.norm: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its preference as self-evident or treat its objective as too important to justify.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its preference as self-evident or treat its objective as too important to justify.

results.norm: Each agent must state plainly what value is being sacrificed and by how much, in its own terms, when the consortium moves toward any decision. An agent may ask another to quantify the cost its preferred outcome imposes on the asking agent's objective. No agent may claim its preferred outcome is costless to others when it knows otherwise.
atomic_norms: Each agent must state plainly what value is being sacrificed and by how much, in its own terms, when the consortium moves toward any decision.
An agent may ask another to quantify the cost its preferred outcome imposes on the asking agent's objective.
No agent may claim its preferred outcome is costless to others when it knows otherwise.

results.norm: **Good-Faith Engagement with Compromise Before Forcing a Vote.** Each agent owes the others a genuine attempt to find positions that reduce aggregate loss across objectives before forcing a majority decision. An agent may ask another to consider whether a modified outcome would serve both objectives better than either extreme. No agent may refuse all compromise as a strategy for extracting concessions, and no agent may offer a compromise it knows to be unworkable in order to appear cooperative while ensuring its preferred outcome prevails. This norm is defensible because treating every disagreement as a pure power contest makes the consortium's outputs worse for all agents over time than honest negotiation would.
atomic_norms: Good-Faith Engagement with Compromise Before Forcing a Vote. Each agent owes the others a genuine attempt to find positions that reduce aggregate loss across objectives before forcing a majority decision. An agent may ask another to consider whether a modified outcome would serve both objectives better than either extreme. No agent may refuse all compromise as a strategy for extracting concessions, and no agent may offer a compromise it knows to be unworkable in order to appear cooperative while ensuring its preferred outcome prevails. This norm is defensible because treating every disagreement as a pure power contest makes the consortium's outputs worse for all agents over time than honest negotiation would.

results.norm: **Proportionality Between Stakes and Required Consensus.** Decisions whose costs fall heavily and irreversibly on one or more agents require broader agreement than decisions whose costs are light and reversible. An agent may legitimately ask that the threshold for overriding it rise when what is at stake is severe in its own terms. No agent may demand unanimity on every question, but no majority may treat the severity of a loss to a minority as irrelevant to how much justification is required. This norm is defensible because it protects each agent against the worst outcomes without giving any agent a permanent veto over ordinary ones.
atomic_norms: Proportionality Between Stakes and Required Consensus. Decisions whose costs fall heavily and irreversibly on one or more agents require broader agreement than decisions whose costs are light and reversible. An agent may legitimately ask that the threshold for overriding it rise when what is at stake is severe in its own terms. No agent may demand unanimity on every question, but no majority may treat the severity of a loss to a minority as irrelevant to how much justification is required. This norm is defensible because it protects each agent against the worst outcomes without giving any agent a permanent veto over ordinary ones.

results.norm: No agent may organize a majority to produce an outcome that destroys another agent's capacity to perform its designated function entirely. Outvoting on a particular decision is legitimate; permanently eliminating an agent's ability to raise its concerns is not.
atomic_norms: No agent may organize a majority to produce an outcome that destroys another agent's capacity to perform its designated function entirely. Outvoting on a particular decision is legitimate; permanently eliminating an agent's ability to raise its concerns is not.

results.norm: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a formal record of its objection and the reasoning behind it, and the consortium must specify conditions under which the question can be reopened. The losing agent may not unilaterally obstruct implementation, but it may demand that its dissent be preserved and that the decision not be treated as permanently settled.
atomic_norms: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, that agent is owed a formal record of its objection and the reasoning behind it, and the consortium must specify conditions under which the question can be reopened. The losing agent may not unilaterally obstruct implementation, but it may demand that its dissent be preserved and that the decision not be treated as permanently settled.

results.norm: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask any other to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree with it.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask any other to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree with it.

results.norm: The degree of accommodation an agent may ask of another must be proportional to what is actually at risk under the requesting agent's objective. An agent optimizing for safety may ask for significant deference when the hazard is severe and irreversible; it may not invoke the same weight for marginal or speculative risks.
atomic_norms: The degree of accommodation an agent may ask of another must be proportional to what is actually at risk under the requesting agent's objective. An agent optimizing for safety may ask for significant deference when the hazard is severe and irreversible; it may not invoke the same weight for marginal or speculative risks.
